era_context: The Hellenistic world in a period of regional consolidation and conflict; the Seleucid Empire fractured; Ptolemaic Egypt under Ptolemy IV; Rome consolidating Italian dominance; China under Qin Shi Huang in the final years of unification consolidation; the Mauryan Empire in India in advanced decline.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most regions outside the Mediterranean and China

events.title: Qin Shi Huang's continued consolidation and the Terracotta Army construction
events.description: During the reign of Qin Shi Huang (221–210 BCE), the construction of the Terracotta Army and the emperor's mausoleum complex began. While the exact dating of the start of construction is uncertain, archaeological evidence suggests that major work on the mausoleum complex was underway during the late 220s BCE. This monumental project reflected the emperor's ambitions for immortality and power, and required the labor of thousands of workers. The project continued until the emperor's death in 210 BCE.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Archaeological excavations of the Terracotta Army and mausoleum complex

events.certainty_note: The Terracotta Army was discovered in 1974 CE. Archaeological dating suggests construction began sometime in the 220s BCE, but the exact year is uncertain. Uncertainty of ±5 years.
cross_references: Part of Qin Shi Huang's broader legacy and the Qin Dynasty (221–206 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: The exact dating of the Terracotta Army's construction is uncertain. Some scholars argue construction began earlier (late 230s BCE), while others place it slightly later (early 220s BCE). The significance of the project as a reflection of Qin power is undisputed, but its specific role in the broader consolidation of the empire is debated.
historiographic_note: 228 BCE is a year of minimal documented events in surviving sources. The Hellenistic sources do not record specific events for this year; the focus is on broader patterns of conflict and consolidation. Chinese sources are available but retrospective. The year appears to be one of gradual consolidation and construction rather than major political or military upheavals. The survival of information about the Terracotta Army is due to modern archaeology rather than ancient written sources.

graph_edges.note: The Terracotta Army construction continued until Qin Shi Huang's death in 210 BCE
